data.title: John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The MSCI World Index, the fund's benchmark, suffered a modest loss in the first quarter.
  • The John Hancock Global Shareholder Yield Fund (MUTF:JGYAX) delivered a positive return and strongly outperformed the benchmark.
  • Dividend-paying stocks outpaced the broader market, creating favorable conditions for the fund due to the nature of our strategy.

Market review and outlook

Uncertainty regarding U.S. tariffs weighed on broad-based global equity indexes in the quarter. Most of the downturn was the result of weakness in the United States, where stocks were hurt by concerns that Washington's trade policies would cause a resurgence in inflation and dampen slower economic growth. The decline in investor sentiment, while a headwind for the benchmark, was primarily confined to U.S. information technology stocks and other higher-growth companies. On the other hand, value stocks, international equities, and dividend payers all outperformed as investors rotated into market segments that had been out of favor. This shift proved to be a significant positive for the fund, particularly when stocks weakened in the second half of the period.

In terms of our outlook, we see inflationary pressure, demand erosion, economic disruptions, and continued market volatility as being potential effects of the recently announced tariffs and the possibility of prolonged trade tensions. The ultimate impact on the real economy and corporate fundamentals is largely dependent on the duration and magnitude of tariffs. The U.S. Federal Reserve is in easing mode, but the path for monetary policy has been clouded by growth pressures and upside risks to inflation stemming from tariffs.

We're cautiously optimistic on the outlook for the economy and equities from a fundamental standpoint, but risk management is essential given the uncertain trade climate and wide array of possible outcomes. In this vein, we believe the fund is poised to capture the productivity of a growing economy while remaining defensively positioned for resilience if growth begins to falter. We maintained a diversified portfolio of high-quality companies with reliable free cash flow growth and longstanding track records of sound capital allocation practices. It's impossible to predict how or when the tariff war will be resolved, but we maintain conviction that our portfolio companies have the ability to adapt to a challenging time and even emerge stronger on the other side. We believe diversification across regions, sectors, and business models provides an additional cushion against uncertainty and volatility.

Contributors and detractors

Stock selection was the primary contributor to relative performance in the quarter. The fund's holdings exceeded or finished in line with the return of the corresponding benchmark components in ten of eleven sectors, with materials being the sole exception. We produced the widest margin of outperformance in communication services, healthcare, and consumer staples. The top contributors at the individual company level generally reflected the defensive nature of the market, with stocks such as Phillip Morris International, Inc. (PM), AbbVie, Inc. (ABBV), and International Business Machines Corp. (IBM) leading the way. A position in CVS Health Corp. (CVS), which rebounded from last year's depressed levels as investors regained confidence in its business, was an additional contributor of note. On the other hand, the five largest detractors were information technology stocks that gave back ground following robust performance in 2024. Among these were three stocks that were hurt by waning enthusiasm about the growth potential of AI: Broadcom, Inc. (AVGO), Hewlett Packard Enterprise Co. (HPE), and Dell Technologies, Inc. (DELL)

Portfolio changes

As is typically the case, our portfolio activity was fairly light in the quarter. We initiated positions in Microchip Technology, Inc. (MCHP) and the Norwegian energy company Equinor ASA (EQNR), and we sold the fund's position in the Canadian telecommunications provider BCE, Inc. (BCE)

data.title: Aurora Innovation: Slow Ramp To Actual Success
data.description:
Aurora Innovation, Inc. (NASDAQ:AUR) achieved a huge accomplishment in the last month, but the company is a long way from actually success. The autonomous trucking company still has a long path to real revenues and a ton of money to raise in the process. My investment thesis is still Bearish on the stock, even after the 35% dip from the last Sell rating.

Slow Ramp Ahead

Just at the end of April, Aurora Innovation officially launched the Aurora Driver autonomous trucking service via the Houston to Dallas lane in Texas. The company started with just one truck and has recently expanded to two trucks operating daily during daylight while avoiding inclement weather.

The whole promise of the service is that going from a few trucks to thousands of trucks is just a matter of time and scaling up. Unfortunately though, Aurora Innovation doesn't plan to scale beyond 10 trucks by the end of the year.

In addition, the Houston to Dallas lane doesn't capture the real benefits of the autonomous trucking service. The real benefit is routes where hours of service limitations restrict drivers from driving more than 11 hours, allowing the Aurora Driver to utilize trucks at a higher rate and make faster deliveries.

Aurora Innovation is working on opening up the key Ft. Worth to El Paso route, followed by the extension to Phoenix. The projection is this 1,000+ mile autonomous lane can be delivered in half the time using the Aurora Driver without service limitations.

Of the 50 billion VMTs on the table for 2028, over 42% of the trips are over 1,000 miles where an autonomous driving service offers considerable benefits. Only 17% of trips are under 300 miles to where a driver can make a round trip without running into service limits requiring spending the night on the road and idling the truck.

Long Path Ahead

An investor can quickly look at the huge jump in revenues forecast for the years ahead and start making a case for paying the $11 billion valuation for the stock. The issue is that the revenues aren't guaranteed and the current goal is only $331 million in 2027.

A couple of examples in the AV space highlight the need for caution. First, Tesla (TSLA) has promised robotaxis for a decade now and the EV company is only promising a launch in June while Waymo, owned by Google (GOOG), already has operations in multiple cities. In this case, the expected AV leader is now lagging the developments in the sector.

The second issue is Cruise Automation, which probably fits more with the risk in Aurora Innovation. The AV company owned by General Motors (GM) had launched robotaxi services in California and an accident not even caused by Cruise led to the company pausing service and never re-starting.

Aurora Innovation targets spending ~$180 million in cash per quarter for the rest of the year while revenues won't ramp towards material amounts until 2027. For this reason, the CFO is guiding towards needing ~$750 million in additional funds to reach cash flow positive in 2028.

On the Q1'25 earnings call, CFO David Maday reiterated the long path ahead to reaching cash flow positive:

In addition to our core growth initiatives, growing the Aurora driver powered fleet, expanding the lanes we operate and unlocking customer endpoints as well as introducing lower cost hardware, we will continue to evaluate opportunities to further advance our competitive position and or de-risk certain elements of our business. Taking these factors into account, we now expect to raise $650 million to $850 million prior to achieving positive free cash flow, which anticipated in 2028.

Aurora Innovation currently has a market cap of nearly $11 billion, so the funds won't be overly dilutive. The biggest issue is having to raise funds at lower stock levels, if the business were to struggle with ramping operations, or ran into a disruptive accident.

Another potential issue is the business model. The company plans to charge by mile for the Aurora Driver services. The Driver-as-a-Service (DAAS) won't be cheap with the company predicting only potentially saving somewhere around 25% of the drivers cost, which amount to an average of $0.97 per mile.

Back on the Q1'25 earnings call, CFO David Maday talked about pricing more around the costs of a human driver:

So we think we’ll deliver tremendous value and that will provide us potential to price somewhat equivalent to a human driven comparison. But even with a slight discount to that, I think we’re going to have really robust margins. And I think there is an opportunity to transform the industry, make it safer and also create tremendous value for everybody and kind of that’s our focus.

Aurora Innovation predicts costs saving to come from fuel efficiency and lower insurance costs. Customers might expect more direct costs savings with fuel efficiency benefits not guaranteed and future competition could attempt to under price the service.

For these reasons, an investor should be skeptical of paying 12x 2028 sales targets with too much potential for error.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Aurora Innovation remains the one autonomous based company priced for success, though the business is only not starting to generate revenues. The company still has a long path to actually being a successful business and investors should wait for a better value to buy the stock.

data.title: Transocean: The Picture Is Getting Clearer, But It's Not Clear Enough
data.description:
Back in February of this year, one company that I decided to reaffirm as a ‘hold’ candidate was offshore drilling business Transocean (NYSE:RIG). At the time, the company was seeing some positive indicators that suggested that future results might improve. On top of this, shares of the company looked quite cheap, particularly on an absolute basis. Even so, I was discouraged by the declining backlog of the business. That meant to me that things had not yet bottomed out. Fast forward to today, and the stock is trading 17% lower than it was back then. That compares to the 0.9% drop experienced by the S&P 500 over the same window of time.

Even though the company is getting tantalizingly cheap, backlog continues to shrink. Management seems optimistic about the long-term outlook for this industry. And under normal circumstances, I would probably claim that now would be a good time to upgrade the stock to a ‘buy’. Unfortunately, the decline in energy prices and broader economic concerns caused by the global trade wars that erupted because of the US create a lot of uncertainty regarding what is certainly a high cost of production for this segment of the energy industry. Because of this, I am not yet ready to upgrade the business. But if shares fall further from here, or if we start to see some additional clarity in the energy industry, an upgrade could be in store before too long.

A look at recent results

Since I last wrote about Transocean, investors have had data come out about one additional operating quarter. This would be the first quarter of the company's 2025 fiscal year. During that time, revenue for the business was $906 million. That's 18.7% above the $763 million the business reported one year earlier. This growth can be attributed to a couple of factors. For starters, the number of operating days for the company’s rigs grew from 1,785 to 1,940, pushing the rig utilization rate for the enterprise up from 53.7% to 63.4%.

This indicates growing demand for the company's services. However, this has materialized in another form as well. During the quarter, the average daily revenue for one of the company’s rigs hit $443,600. That's the highest it has hit in several years and represents a meaningful increase over the $408,200 reported one year earlier. The company also benefited to the tune of $30 million from increased activity associated with its newbuild ultra-deepwater floater Deepwater Aquila. All combined, this translates to a positive environment for the offshore drilling industry.

Even though revenue for the company improved, the business went from a net profit of $98 million to a net loss of $79 million. Even on an adjusted basis, it worsened from a net loss of $22 million to a net loss of $65 million. The big contributor to this weakening was the fact that, in the first quarter of 2024, the company enjoyed an income tax benefit of $191 million. That compares to a $39 million expense incurred this year. If we look at before tax profits, the company actually improved significantly from a loss of $93 million to a loss of $40 million. Other profitability metrics ended up coming in mixed. For instance, operating cash flow went from negative $86 million to positive $26 million. But if we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get a decline from $144 million to $99 million. Meanwhile, EBITDA for the business improved from $199 million to $244 million.

We don't really know what to expect when it comes to 2025 in its entirety. Management did say that revenue should come in at between $3.85 billion and $3.95 billion. At the midpoint, that would represent a 10.7% rise over the $3.52 billion the business generated last year. In all likelihood, profits and cash flows will rise as well. But because this is a pretty volatile space anyways, it would be unwise to project out much from there. Instead, if we use results from 2023 and 2024, we can see in the chart above how the stock is currently priced. These are multiples that I consider to be appealing. While the price to adjusted operating cash flow multiple is particularly low, in light of the high amount of debt the business has, $5.96 billion on a net basis, I think it would be better for investors to give more weight to the EV to EBITDA multiple.

Company Price / Operating Cash Flow EV / EBITDA
Transocean 2.8 7.0
Seadrill Limited (SDRL) 48.7 3.2
Patterson-UTI Energy (PTEN) 2.2 18.4
Noble Corporation (NE) 4.5 4.8
Helmerich & Payne (HP) 2.7 4.2
Valaris Limited (VAL) 5.4 4.5

In the table above, I then decided to compare Transocean to five similar companies. It seems to be more or less in the middle of the group. I say this because, on a price to operating cash flow basis, two of the five competitors are trading at levels that are lower than it is. But when it comes to the EV to EBITDA approach, four of the five are trading lower. Considering that we are giving more weight to the latter, one could even argue that the stock is perhaps a bit pricey compared to the competition.

Long term, management seems optimistic. For instance, they expect global deepwater investment to rise by 10% this year. And from 2026 to 2027, they expected to grow another 16%. And by 2030, they expect annual deepwater investment to be 40% higher than it is today. Some of the near term success that the company is banking on includes the expectation that rig demand in Brazil generated by Petrobras (PBR) is expected to grow to over 30 active rigs by the end of this year and should grow even further next year. It just so happens that Petrobras is the company's second largest customer, accounting for 21% of total revenue last year. So its success should be the success of Transocean as well.

It might seem unlikely that demand for offshore drilling could grow so significantly over the next few years. However, management ties this to a couple of factors. For starters, between 2023 and 2030, total global oil production is expected to climb by 13% from 100 million barrels per day to 113 million barrels per day. But the actual amount of oil that will need to be extracted is far greater than a 13 million barrel per day rise. This is because depletion is expected to take 43 million barrels per day off of the space, which means that total new production of 56 million barrels per day will be needed. Management also claims that at prices of $50 per barrel or greater, the offshore industry is viable. By comparison, for the prolific Permian Basin, we are looking at between $62 per barrel and $64 per barrel, depending on whether we are talking about the Midland Basin or the Delaware Basin.

As great as this inheritance is, there are certain challenges that the company is going to face. For starters, backlog continues to fall. At the end of the first quarter, backlog was $7.93 billion. That was down from the $8.33 billion reported at the end of last year and it compares to the $8.86 billion reported one year ago. Seeing as how backlog is a leading indicator of future revenue, it is disconcerting that this metric has not at least bottomed out as of yet. Once it does, I will feel a lot more optimistic.

Another issue relates to the overall net debt of the business. It has dropped nicely from $6.67 billion in the first quarter of 2023 to $5.94 billion in the final quarter of last year. It did tick up modestly, however, to $5.96 billion in the first quarter of 2025. Obviously, we can't expect every quarter to experience improvements in an industry that is known for volatile cash flow figures. But I would still like to see management bring overall net debt down as quickly as possible. Unfortunately, because the market capitalization of the firm is $2.13 billion, it can't really do much with the net debt in terms of issuing equity. Its only options are to use excess cash flows and/or proceeds from asset sales. As of the end of the first quarter of 2025, the company had $344 million in assets held for sale in the form of two of its ultra deepwater floaters. Those were originally under contract to be sold, but that contract has now been terminated because the buyer has not been able or willing to make payment.

Another issue at hand here is the supply and demand environment for oil. Because OPEC+ is now raising production in order to try to bring some of its members to heel when it comes to their commitments to cut output, and because the global trade war that the US started under the Trump Administration, oil prices have been on the decline. From the time I last wrote about Transocean earlier this year until today, Brent crude prices have dropped from $74.76 per barrel to $64.81 per barrel. This still means that offshore drilling is profitable according to management. However, if the economy is heading in the direction that I think it is, I wouldn't be surprised to see further weakening in this space in the near term. That would certainly put a damper on investment decisions for companies that have, in recent years, been hesitant to make big commitments on projects that will take a while and tremendous amounts of capital to get up and running. The fact that interest rates remain elevated because of inflationary concerns caused by the tariff situation also makes it more expensive for companies to operate. So that's a negative as well.

Takeaway

It does appear to me as though the future, if we focus on the long haul, is looking better for Transocean. However, there are certain near-term concerns that investors should be aware of. The company is showing some nice growth on the top line and some of its bottom-line figures are improving. However, net debt has ticked up recently and backlog continues to shrink. In light of this mixed picture, I believe that maintaining a ‘hold’ rating on the company is the right choice at the moment.

data.title: abrdn Emerging Markets Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fund performance

The Fund returned 0.26% over the quarter, lagging the benchmark by 267 basis points (bps). Negative stock selection in China and Taiwan were the key drivers of the underperformance, while selection effects in India added value.[1]

The performance data quoted represents past performance and current returns may be lower or higher. Class A shares have up to a 5.75% front-end sales charge and a 0.25% 12b-1 fee.

The investment return and principal value will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less

than the original cost. To obtain performance information current to the most recent month-end, which may be higher or lower than the performance shown above, please call 866-667-9231 or go to aberdeeninvestments.com/us/literature.

Total returns assume the reinvestment of all distributions. Total returns may reflect a waiver of part of the Fund’s fees for certain periods since inception, without which returns would have been lower. Indexes are unmanaged and provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index.

Despite tariff uncertainty, China started 2025 with a very strong quarter. Gains were fuelled largely by DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) announcement, which prompted a wave of AI-driven optimism and increased investor confidence in China’s ability to innovate beyond tech sanctions. This triggered a sharp rally in the shares of Alibaba, given that it has the largest cloud business in the country and is therefore viewed as a key beneficiary of these AI-related developments.

Tencent, which has the second-largest cloud business in China, was another standout performer. PICC Property and Casualty (OTCPK:PPCCF) also fared well after posting a positive set of full-year results. However, not holding Xiaomi (OTCPK:XIACF) and BYD (OTCPK:BYDDF), which are seen by the market as indirect AI beneficiaries and beneficiaries of government policy, proved costly. Xiaomi is a direct beneficiary of the consumption trade-in programme put forward by the government. Additionally, our mainland-listed A-share companies trailed as both onshore and offshore investors rushed to invest in AI beneficiaries listed offshore in Hong Kong. As a result, we underperformed in China by about 140 bps.

Semiconductor and tech hardware stocks came under pressure following the DeepSeek announcement as investors speculated that more efficient generative AI models might lead to lower demand and capex in the tech value chain and, ultimately, lower growth. Revisions in capex and earnings from some US companies have crystalised concerns. Consequently, our Taiwanese holdings in Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Co (TSM), Accton Technology, Delta Electronics, Yageo Corp and MediaTek (OTCPK:MDTTF) detracted and were responsible for around half of the underperformance during the quarter, though not holding Hon Hai Precision Industry mitigated the losses.

Outside Asia, the off-benchmark position in Latin America-based IT services company Globant (GLOB) was another main drag on performance. The company’s fourth-quarter results were in line with expectations, but the official 2025 guidance indicated a softening of expected demand in its core market, the US. In South Africa, gold miners rallied strongly as gold continued to touch new highs. Our lack of exposure to a number of these names was negative.

On the flip side, stock selection in India was robust. Core holdings such as Power Grid Corporation of India and Indian Hotels rose sharply in March as the central bank’s liquidity boosting measures and a well-received budget from the government seemed to lift sentiment.

Investments in the United Arab Emirates, Abu Dhabi Islamic Bank and Aldar Properties, contributed further to relative returns.

Market review

Emerging market equities outpaced developed markets in the first quarter as the consensus around US exceptionalism began to unwind. The MSCI Emerging Markets Index rose by 3.01% in US dollar terms. Political developments in the wake of US President Donald Trump’s inauguration in January dominated sentiment, particularly his tariff plans and the global fallout. The US Federal Reserve paused its interest rate cuts amid increased economic uncertainty. Concerns that tariffs could derail US growth also weighed on the US dollar, which boosted some markets.

Among major markets, China led returns despite escalating tariffs from the US. Chinese start-up DeepSeek’s model that was trained without the most advanced chips. Separately, policymakers committed to more fiscal stimulus and set a 2025 growth target of around 5% during the annual parliamentary gathering.

Indian equities were dragged down by the slowing economy for most of the quarter. In March, the central bank’s measures to add liquidity and improving economic indicators began to revive investor confidence.

Elsewhere, Gulf bourses closed mixed on concerns over oil prices. Latin America was buoyed by gains in Brazil and Mexico. Brazilian stocks rebounded amid sentiment that the market was oversold, while Mexico has thus far effectively navigated the tariff conflict with the US.

Activity

We added three holdings in China. Commercial lender China Merchants Bank conducts business domestically and internationally. Li Auto (LI) designs, develops and sells new energy vehicles. Shenzhen Envicool Technology is a provider of temperature control systems. We also bought India-based J.B. Chemicals and Pharmaceuticals, which makes pharmaceuticals, chemicals and agro-based products.

Conversely, we sold Credicorp (BAP), Ping An Insurance (OTCPK:PNGAY), Saudi Arabian Oil Co and Sungrow.

Outlook

Consensus heading into 2025 was one of US exceptionalism with tax cuts, deregulation and tech dominance leading US and global equities higher. However, with valuations as stretched as they had been heading into the year, there was scope for a downside surprise. The emergence of Chinese AI start-up DeepSeek, combined with a tariff-inclined Trump, appears to have provided the downside surprise.

However, this environment still offers reasons for optimism for emerging market investors.

At the time of writing, Trump has announced his tariffs. They set a 10% baseline for all countries with more punitive reciprocal tariffs for identified nations, though most of the higher tariffs have now been pushed back, except for China, which has hit back at the US with levies of its own. Overall, the tariffs answer some key questions and suggest that Trump’s goals of shifting manufacturing and raising revenue for tax cuts are prime and centre.

Tariffs on China were stacked, but we believe the key driver for Chinese equities will continue to be the domestic environment, where the outlook remains more positive, with further support likely in the coming months. India is well-positioned to navigate a more fractured global economy, backed by long-term structural drivers and IT service exports, which are not currently under fire.

Emerging market valuations are still attractive, both historically and compared to the US. Our portfolio companies are delivering results, which we believe the market will reward. We remain confident in our holdings and their ability to navigate market crosswinds.

Footnote

[1] The MSCI Emerging Markets Net Index is an unmanaged index considered representative of stocks of developing countries. Indexes are unmanaged and have been provided for comparison purposes only. No fees or expenses are reflected. You cannot invest directly in an index.

data.title: Trees Don't Grow To The Sky, But Nvidia Does
data.description:
Nvidia Corporation (NASDAQ:NVDA) recently released its first quarter fiscal 2026 (calendar 2025) earnings. The company managed to outpace market expectations and maintained its growth on a satisfactory level, while risks for the company seem to be manageable so far. Despite that revenue growth remained robust, gross margins took a big hit due to China-related export restrictions, which remain one of the main risks for the foreseeable future.

Nonetheless, nothing seems to threaten Nvidia's dominance in the AI infrastructure segment at the moment. Thus, I reiterate my Buy rating for the company as my bullish case stays intact.

Earnings Overview

Nvidia delivered an impressive revenue growth of 69% year-over-year, beating analyst expectations by $810 million. However, the biggest caveat of the report is a huge drop in gross margin, which contracted to 60.5% from 73% a quarter ago and from 78.4% a year ago.

Operating expenses grew by 44% to $5.03 billion, reflecting significant R&D investments. The company expects full-year fiscal 2026 operating expense growth to be in the mid-30% range, so we can expect stabilization of expenses at lower levels than we've seen in Q1 FY26. As for net income, it contracted by 15% Q/Q to $18 billion due to export restrictions on supplies of AI chips to China.

Now let's take a look at the company's segment performance.

The Data Center segment generated $39.1 bn in revenue, up 10% from the previous quarter and an impressive 73% year-over-year. The segment remains the primary growth engine for Nvidia, accounting for nearly 89% of total revenue. The demand for AI infrastructure remains strong, with Nvidia's CEO Jensen Huang noting that "AI inference token generation has surged tenfold in just one year."

The Gaming segment also had an exceptional quarter, reaching record revenue of $3.8 bn, up 48% Q/Q and 42% Y/Y. The launch of the new RTX 5000 series of cards was quite controversial, to say the least, though it didn't stop the company from achieving great results.

Professional Visualization remained relatively flat at $509 million, showing a relatively modest 19% year-over-year increase.

Automotive revenue was $567 million, down slightly from the previous quarter but still up 72% year-over-year, indicating continued momentum in the autonomous vehicle and automotive AI space.

Nvidia's balance sheet remains robust, with cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities amounting to $53.7 billion, up from $43.2 billion in the previous quarter. The company generated $27.4 billion in operating cash flow and $26.1 billion in free cash flow, demonstrating the cash-generative nature of the business even amid the current challenges.

Export Restrictions To Continue Crippling Earnings Growth

The most significant development this quarter was the U.S. government's decision on April 9, 2025, to require export licenses for shipping Nvidia's H20 chips to China. This policy change forced Nvidia to take a massive $4.5 billion charge for excess inventory as a chunk of China-related demand essentially disappeared overnight.

For Q2, Nvidia expects to lose approximately $8 billion in H20 revenue due to export controls, which honestly looks like a somewhat conservative estimate to me. If the Trump administration manages to strike a trade deal with China, then most likely at least some of the export restrictions may be lifted.

For reference, H20 chips were specifically designed for the Chinese market, making the export restrictions particularly painful for Nvidia. The company's ability to supply these chips elsewhere remains uncertain given that H20 chips are much less powerful than the most recent Blackwell chips.

Without the H20 charge, Nvidia's gross margins would have been much more impressive. GAAP gross margin came in at 60.5%, while non-GAAP gross margin was 61.0%. However, excluding the $4.5 billion H20-related charge, the non-GAAP gross margin would have been 71.3%, which is more in line with what investors have come to expect from Nvidia.

Valuation

At current levels, Nvidia trades at approximately 31.6x forward P/E based on J.P. Morgan's FY26 EPS estimate of $4.27. JPM's assumptions are the following:

  • Earnings CAGR of 30-35% over the next few years;
  • Continued market domination in the high-growth AI infrastructure segment;
  • Potential for strong revenue growth from automotive (with the current $14 billion pipeline) and software/licensing segments;
  • Strong free cash flow generation.

JPM maintains a $170 price target for the stock, implying 30-35x FY26 earnings, which seems reasonable, in my opinion, given the company's growth trajectory and market leadership position.

Also, it's worthwhile to take a look at valuations across semiconductor stocks.

According to JPM estimates, Nvidia trades at 30.5x 2025E P/E, which represents significant multiple compression from its 2024 ratio of 45.0x. In the meantime, Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) trades at 28.2x forward P/E with a much smaller revenue scale, while Marvell Technology (NASDAQ:MRVL) trades at 22.6x forward P/E despite generating only $6.3 billion in expected 2025 revenue versus Nvidia's $159 billion. AMD (NASDAQ:AMD), Nvidia's key competitor, trades at 27.9x forward P/E, making Nvidia's 30.5x multiple only modestly higher than its closest AI competitor, despite Nvidia's superior market position and larger scale of its business.

Therefore, even though Nvidia's valuation is elevated, it's not dramatically out of line with other growth-oriented semiconductor companies.

Performance Recap

Since my previous bullish article about Nvidia, the company's shares are up 12%, outperforming the S&P 500 Index (SP500) by a huge margin. Despite the "bumpy road" for the stock over the last few months, I think that Nvidia's fundamentals are well aligned with the stock growth trajectory.

I consider Nvidia's recent performance as a great example even despite its rich valuation and negative market outlook in general, high-quality businesses may keep outperforming the broader market.

The Bottom Line

So far, Nvidia remains an undisputed leader in key areas, from data centers and AI to gaming and autonomous driving. Neither the potential competition from AMD with its AI chips nor the "DeepSeek drama" (DEEPSEEK) have meaningfully affected AI-related demand, which continues to accelerate with further adoption of AI driven by companies like OpenAI.

In this regard, the only medium-term risks for Nvidia's bullish case I see at the moment are a potential expansion of export restrictions and deteriorating macro outlook due to the continuous implementation of tariffs by the Trump administration. Both factors, however, do not represent a long-term threat to Nvidia's business, in my opinion, so I reiterate my Buy rating for the stock.

data.title: FLIN: India's Growth Story Is Only Gaining Pace
data.description:
The Franklin FTSE India ETF (NYSEARCA:FLIN) has been one of my favored low-cost India plays for a few years now. Even through the recent downturn, I’ve stayed bullish on this increasingly popular ETF (see FLIN: Don't Write Off India Just Yet). This contrarian view is starting to pay off - since I last called a potential ‘bottom’ in Indian large-caps (see NFTY: India Enters The 'Panic Zone'), India has emerged as among the best performers in the emerging market universe. Note that this comes despite ongoing trade uncertainties and, more recently, an escalation of India-Pakistan border tensions (see INDY: Look Through India's Border Tensions).

The question now, though, is whether FLIN remains a buy, particularly in the context of re-rated large-cap earnings multiples at ~23x – not too far off last September’s peak. In this article, I’ll do a stock take on where we stand and outline some very significant catalysts in the India pipeline.

A Trillion Rupee RBI Catalyst

For much of last year, India’s banking system struggled through a liquidity deficit situation. That led to pressure on funding costs, which along with a wave of ‘regulatory tightening’ measures by the last Reserve Bank of India (the ‘RBI’ or India’s central bank), kept a lid on system lending growth. The regulatory tone has shifted, however, since new RBI Governor Malhotra took over (see ICICI Bank: Full Steam Ahead For India's Banking Outperformer and HDFC Bank: Asia's Compounding Machine Is Back On Track) and the monetary policy focus has well and truly turned accommodative in recent months.

While the headlines have been focused on policy rate cuts, it’s also worth double-clicking on last week’s larger-than-anticipated RBI dividend and the implications for system liquidity. Per the accompanying press release, this fiscal year saw an INR 2.7tn payout (~0.7% of GDP) to the Central Government – well above last year’s INR 2.1tn (~0.6% of GDP). In isolation, an incremental 0.1% of GDP might not seem like all that much. But combine this with the RBI’s very sizeable liquidity injections this year and you have a scenario where this incremental INR2-3tn moves the needle quite significantly for the system.

All this is great news for a rate-sensitive ETF like FLIN. On the one hand, it brings down the Indian cost of money (see declining Indian bond yields and the recent spread compression vs US Treasuries below), which, in turn, boosts equity valuations across the board. Lower funding costs also bodes well for economic activity; moreso for rate-sensitive sectors like Financials and Consumer Discretionary, both of which FLIN heavily indexes toward.

On the Right Side of a Cyclical Swing

Now, the funding boost comes at a time when India may be entering a cyclical upswing. Key leading indicators like the manufacturing PMI (up to ~58) and services PMI (up to ~59) both stand out relative to its emerging market peers – note second-best Indonesia’s manufacturing PMI is only tracking at 52.4 amid ongoing trade uncertainties.

As for the recent March quarter earnings season, we’ve already seen more beats than misses for the mega-caps – a great sign considering Jan-March was a lower growth/ higher inflation period. Expect things to only get better from here, as decelerating food inflation and lower oil prices, as well as an above-par monsoon (per IMD forecasts) filter through to earnings. Don’t rule out a bigger fiscal or monetary policy boost either - especially if the current growth/inflation dynamic persists.

In sum, I see no reason why Indian large-caps don’t average back into a mid-teens % earnings growth range (in line with consensus) going forward. This growth, coupled with an expanding economic value added spread (i.e., the gap between India's return and cost of capital), should more than justify a >20x forward multiple, in my view.

Watch Out for the Upcoming Trade Boost

Of course, there’s also the big external overhang – tariffs. Since ‘Liberation Day’, the Trump administration has softened its stance quite significantly; firstly, by allowing for temporary exemptions through July and secondly, by taking on an increasingly conciliatory approach in its talks with key adversary, China. This week’s decision by the Court of International Trade to block ‘Liberation Day’ tariffs also helps – even if the administration has ways around the ruling.

To be clear, outside of pharmaceuticals, India isn’t as directly exposed to US goods trade, so relatively speaking, the overhang isn’t quite as large. Nevertheless, India is exposed to more indirect second-order effects, so a trade deal, which media reports suggest is on the horizon, could still be a major near-term catalyst for FLIN.

The more interesting question for me, though, is India’s ability to emerge as a net beneficiary longer-term. India has always had high trade barriers of its own, so a trade deal with concessions, as hinted by President Trump (“It’s very hard to sell into India, and they’ve offered us a deal where, basically, they’re willing to literally charge us no tariff”), isn’t necessarily a positive.

Key will be where these concessions come in - cuts to premium liquor and EV duties, for instance, would be a lot more symbolic than to a politically-sensitive area like agriculture. Also, what India gets in return and whether it can build on its “Make in India” momentum as global supply chains shift away from China.

In the meantime, the recent UK-India free trade agreement, which saw 99% of Indian exports gain duty-free status, offers a positive read through. For now, my base case remains that upcoming trade deals with the US and EU will re-rate FLIN higher.

Summing Up

India’s macro outlook screens very favorably; so does its earnings growth path, which looks on track to hit a mid-teen % pace for the years to come. Relative to this fundamental backdrop, as well as a catalyst-rich next few months, Indian large-caps don’t look all that expensive. Net, ETF cost leader, FLIN, remains a great way to gain exposure to the Indian growth story.

data.title: FLIN: Monsoons Have Lifted The Prospects Of This Competitive India ETF
data.description:
A Lot More Mainstream Now, But An Underwhelming Start To The Year

We last covered the Franklin FTSE India ETF (NYSEARCA:FLIN) back in October 2022, when it was not quite in the limelight with only around $50m in AUM; in roughly 30 months, things have changed quite considerably, with its AUM swelling to over$2200m (although the price performance has been less impressive, with an expansion of only ~35%).

While its progress over the past few years is quite commendable, FLIN, which is a passively managed product that tracks the FTSE India Capped Index, hasn’t quite got going in 2025, generating minuscule returns of just 3%, and underperforming both global markets, as well as its EM peers.

Best-In-Class Cost Competitiveness Is Still A Massive Boon

Notwithstanding the sober start to the year, FLIN, which currently covers around 259 stocks, still deserves to be under consideration for those seeking diversified exposure to Indian equities, because it offers the cheapest route to this promising terrain. For context, our ETF in focus is priced at an expense ratio of just 0.19%, which is just one-fourth the cost of the average expense ratio of other Indian-themed ETFs (and around 30bps lower than the median ETF rate). Put another way, a $1000 investment in FLIN, will only set you back by less than $2, whereas in the case of other Indian ETFs, you’d lose out on around $8 by way of expenses. Long-term investors will also be happy to note that this is a product that offers a great deal of stability, as roughly only around 10% of its holdings gets turned over every year (which is only around one-third of the churn that most ETFs witness every year).

The Fastest Growing Largest Economy In The World, Gets Another Fillip From The Rain Gods

India’s current GDP growth backdrop may not be as dazzling as the average annual growth rate of 9% seen from FY22 to FY24, but investors shouldn’t forget that the largest democracy in the world is still expected to be the fastest growing major economy over the next couple of years. At expected growth rate of 6.2-6.3% in the current year and next, India is still expected to comfortably outpace the globe by over 2x, and also exceed emerging markets by over 150bps.

We suspect there could be upside risks to India’s growth forecasts, as the central bank there is attempting to make financing conditions a lot more palatable. Next week, benchmark rates are expected to be cut by 25bps for the third straight time, and that won’t be all, as another cut is expected in August as well. The RBI is likely feeling emboldened to go down this easing route, as inflation in India (at 3.16%), is on a downward trajectory (well below the bank’s comfort point of 4%), and at its lowest point in almost 6 years!

Risks of inflation picking up, particularly on the food inflation front, looks quite unlikely, given that the Indian Meteorological Department (IMD) had -earlier this week- reiterated its expectations of “above normal” monsoon rainfall during June to September ( 106% of the long period average). Besides helping keep a lid on prices (by way of higher farm output as this monsoon is believed to supply 70% of the rain water needed for India’s crops), a solid monsoon, also raises the prospects of better purchasing power in the hands of rural India. For added context, do consider that 60% of India’s population still comes from rural India, and the agriculture sector employs 40% of its total workforce. A better income backdrop for the rural sector should also help ameliorate some of the bad loan pressures that Indian banks (who account for the largest share of FLIN's portfolio at 29%) have been experiencing over the years.

The Relative Valuation Picture Versus The Globe Is More Comforting Now

In the past, our overriding reason not to pursue Indian equities was the sky-high valuations relative to other pockets across the globe. Well, that narrative no longer holds, with the FLIN now trading at a near-identical weighted average P/E multiple as global equities (represented by the Vanguard Total World Stock ETF or VT).

Crucially, at around a multiple of 17x, FLIN’s holdings offer much superior long-term earnings growth of over 25%, which implies a compelling price-to-long-term-earnings growth ratio of just 0.68x. In contrast, at a roughly similar P/E, global stocks are only poised to deliver long-term earnings growth of less than 10% (a steeper implied PEG of 1.76x).

Around a year ago, those who rotate in and out of various global pockets based on steep and low relative strength ratios, may have been put off by the prospect of pursuing an overheated Indian terrain and may even have been tempted to short equities there. For instance, FLIN’s strength vs global stocks were at record highs of around 0.37x. That ratio no longer looks overextended, and has almost mean-reverted to the long-term average of ~0.31x.

A Useful Yield To Help During Potential Drawdowns

Another incentive to load up on FLIN now is that its distribution profile is looking quite attractive. FLIN distributes on a semi-annual basis, and over the last two years, the annual payouts have grown at an impressive CAGR of 68% (last year’s hike was up by 34.3%). As a result of the increased generosity, FLIN, at current prices, is yielding quite a competitive figure of 1.54%, which is 37bps more than its 5-year average. Crucially, its current yield is substantially higher than the two largest Indian ETFs- the MSCI India ETF (INDA), and the WisdomTree India Earnings ETF (EPI), both of whom are currently offering unappealing figures that are lower than average.

Some Positive Technical Developments

We’ve also been enthused to note FLIN’s fire-fighting qualities at the lower levels. Take a look at the image below which showcases FLIN’s monthly movements over the last six-and-a-half years. What’s evident is that this ETF has trended higher, within the boundaries of an ascending channel (marked by the two black lines).

In February this year, it looked like we were going to see the end of the channel, with the price breaking down below it, but that breakdown proved to be short-lived, with bargain hunting support sending the ETF back above the lower channel boundary (note also that the last couple of months have seen candles with relatively long lower wicks, reiterating the buying pressure at lower levels). Given that the price is now close to its channel support, and still a long way of the upper channel boundary, we view the risk-reward as very healthy.

Supporting all this, we’ve also recently seen FLIN recoup its landmark 200DMA for the first time in 5 months, which should help tilt even nore bullish sentiment in its favor.

Conclusion- Rating Upgrade

We were always fans of FLIN’s structural qualities, but given the more palatable valuation backdrop, a potentially better macro environment, and good risk-reward on the charts, we revise our rating on this ETF from a hold to a buy.

data.title: Phillip Morris's Undeniable Market Leadership Comes At Too Great A Cost
data.description:
PM's Rally Has Occurred Overly Fast & Furious - Minimal Margin Of Safety

We previously covered Philip Morris (NYSE:PM) in March 2025, discussing how its premium valuations had been justified by the recent US FDA approval for ZYN, attributed to the Nicotine Pouches segment's undeniable growth driver prospects.

Combined with the robust smoke-free growth across IQOS's growing users/ market share, the richer overall operating margins, and the healthier balance sheet, we had believed that the stock was likely to outperform the wider market and its tobacco peers, as it emerged as the undisputed king in the tobacco market.

PM 1Y Stock Price

Since that Buy rating, PM has already charted a further +18% stock price rally, well outperforming the wider market at +5.2% and its tobacco peers in varying degrees.

Much of the tailwinds are naturally attributed to its undeniable market leadership in the tobacco market across combustibles and smoke-free approaches, with its cigarettes sales volume growing by +1.1% YoY in FQ1'25, Heated Tobacco Units growing by +11.9% YoY, and Oral Smoke-Free Product volumes growing by +31% YoY.

These numbers are despite the tougher YoY comparisons from FY2024 levels of +0.6% YoY, +11.5% YoY, and +27.8% YoY and a year ago at -0.4% YoY, +20.9% YoY, and +40% YoY, respectively, with it underscoring its ability to drive renewed growth opportunity despite the secular decline of tobacco demand.

Combined with the management's higher pricing actions, it is unsurprising that PM has been able to report robust combustible revenue growth by +3.8% YoY in FQ1'25 and smoke-free revenue growth by +20.4% YoY, along with combustible gross profit growth by +5.3% YoY and smoke-free gross profit growth by +33.1% YoY, respectively.

These have already triggered the tobacco company's robust total revenue growth to $9.3B (-4.1% QoQ/ +10.2% YoY organically), richer operating margins to 40.7% (+4.4 points QoQ/ +2.5 YoY/ +2.3 from FY2019 levels of 35.3%), and adj EPS of $1.76 (+18.1% QoQ/ +3.5% YoY).

Much of PM's tailwinds are naturally attributed to "the powerful growth and scale effect" of IQOS across its growing adoption and manufacturing efficiency, as observed in the robust "HTU growth across all regions with share of over 18% in airports where IQOS is present."

With the company already launching IQOS 3 devices/ HTUs in Austin, Texas since March 2025, we believe that its long-term prospects remain highly promising, with it likely to be significantly boosted by the speculative US FDA approval for IOQS ILUMA by sometime in H2'25.

This is on top of PM's highly strategic acquisition of ZYN from Swedish Match in 2022, with the nicotine pouches already reporting 202M cans in FQ1'25 US shipments (+22.4% QoQ/ +53% YoY), thanks to the growing production capacity and robust consumer demand.

The management has also been looking to expand its production capacity in Owensboro plant, with further sequential growth likely, as they also raise the FY2025 volume sold guidance to 820M cans (+27.3% YoY), compared to the prior guidance of 800M (+24.2% YoY).

Given that ZYN remains the only nicotine pouch approved by the US FDA, we believe that they may continue to command leading market share moving forward, based on the Q1'25 category value share at over 70%, as reported by Nielsen.

The Consensus Forward Estimates

These reasons may also be why the consensus forward estimates remain promising, with PM expected to report an accelerated top/ bottom-line growth at a CAGR of +7.6%/ +11.5% through FY2027, despite the management's reiterated FY2025 guidance.

This is compared to the original estimates of +4.5%/ +6.5%, 5Y historical growth profile at a CAGR of +4.9%/ +4.8%, and 10Y growth at +2.4%/ +2.7%, respectively, with it underscoring why its well diversified and highly successful smoke-free approaches have been top/ bottom-line accretive.

The same has been observed in PM's robust net cash provided by (used in) operating activities of $11.62B over the LTM (+11.7% YoY) and $12.21B in FY2024 (+32.7% YoY), allowing them to service their annualized dividend obligations of $8.48B (+4.4% YoY).

This reason is also why we are not overly concerned about its deteriorating balance sheet with a growing net debt level of -$45.13B (-8.8% QoQ/ -8.8% YoY/ -86.7% from FY2019 levels of -$24.17B), with the growing profitability already triggering the moderation of its net debt to EBITDA ratio to 2.80x in FQ1'25, lower than the 3.41x reported in FQ1'24 while nearing the 2.29x reported in FY2019.

PM Valuations

This reason is also why PM has been upgraded to FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 23.85x, up from the 1Y mean of 18.71x, 5Y mean of 16.60x, and the 10Y mean of 17.31x, with the stock seemingly elevated compared to the sector median of 16.36x and its direct tobacco peers, including Altria (MO) at 11.10x, British American Tobacco p.l.c. (BTI) at 9.87x, and Imperial Brands (OTCQX:IMBBY) (OTCQX:IMBBF) at 9.17x.

Despite PM's accelerated bottom-line growth profile, it is apparent that the stock remains somewhat reasonably valued at FWD PEG non-GAAP ratio of 2.10x, compared to its 5Y mean of 1.95x and its direct tobacco peers, including MO at 2.91x, BTI at 2.34x, and IMBBY at 1.13x.

These developments underscore PM's decent investment thesis at current levels, albeit requiring a moderate pullback for an improved return profile, one that we shall further discuss in the next segment.

So, Is PM Stock A Buy, Sell, or Hold?

PM 7Y Stock Price

For now, PM has charted new peaks of $179s ranges as the stock continues to run away from its 50/ 100/ 200 day moving averages, with it naturally buoyed by the overly greedy market sentiments as the undisputed tobacco market leader.

Based on the management's reiterated FY2025 adj EPS guidance of $7.32 at the midpoint (+11.4% YoY ex currency) and the 10Y P/E mean of 17.31x, it is apparent that the stock is trading at a notable premium against our updated fair value estimates of $126.70.

Based on the consensus raised FY2027 adj EPS estimates from $8.62 to $9.11, PM has also pulled forward all of its upside potential to our base-case long-term price target of $157.70.

The same has been observed in its moderated forward dividend yields of 3.03%, compared to the 5Y averages of 4.99% and its tobacco peers, including MO at 6.83%, BTI at 6.63%, and IMBBY at 5.33%, with it underscoring why the rally may have occurred overly fast and furious.

Assuming that PM is able to sustain its market leadership while holding on to its premium FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 23.85x, there appears to be a decent upside potential of +21.1% to our bull-case long-term price target of $217.20.

While the stock may potentially generate new heights ahead, in our opinion, valuations and fundamentals do matter for investors seeking long-term capital appreciation prospects, with the expensive valuations and sky-high stock prices implying a relatively risky form of momentum trading during an uncertain macroeconomic environment.

As a result, we prefer to downgrade the PM stock to a Hold (Neutral) here, with interested investors recommended to observe the price movement for a little longer before adding, especially given the potential volatility arising from the drastically higher short interest volumes by +50.6% on a YoY basis.

data.title: EastGroup Properties: Recycling Rents To Drive Growth
data.description:
In the spring of last year, I called EastGroup Properties (NYSE:EGP) a fully valued industrial Sunbelt play. The company enjoyed an outperformance at the time thanks to low leverage and upbeat remarks on the business performance.

While the positioning of the sector and Sunbelt states looked sound, I struggled with a focus of management on absolute growth, rather than growth per share, as quite some dilution has been incurred over time.

While focus on absolute growth, rather than growth per share, remains a concern, I am getting more upbeat amidst rising implied cap rates, which combined with substantial re-leasing spreads makes shares likely a reasonable store of value here.

A Sunbelt Industrial REIT

Founded back in 1969, EastGroup is a REIT which focuses on key states like Florida, Arizona, Texas, California, and some others. Texas, Florida and California combined make up 75% of the assets.

The company provides functional and flexible space with spaces typically ranging between 20,000 and 100,000 square feet, located alongside transportation hubs in supply constrained markets. These are not the big buildings, as the company claims that these relative smaller units traditionally benefit from lower vacancy rates, in part with fewer of such buildings being developed in recent years.

Over the past 55 years, the company has grown the portfolio to 63 million square feet, located in the Sunbelt states which enjoy above-average GDP and population growth. The portfolio is built up through asset purchases and own developments, located near cities and often in park settings.

The fact that the company focuses on smaller buildings make that the tenant base is very diversified, supporting a dividend which has been paid out, and often increased for over 3 decades in a row.

A Boom-Bust

A $50 stock in 2015 rose to the $100 mark in 2020 as shares broke the $200 mark in 2021, aided by the boom in e-commerce following the pandemic. Ever since, shares have largely traded in a $150-$200 range, now trading at $166 per share.

In the meantime, the company has grown sales, for roughly 99% comprised out of rental revenues, from $230 million in 2015 to about $650 million on a trailing basis, making that revenues have nearly increased by a factor of 3 times. Much of this growth has been financed with dilution, as the shares count has risen more than 50% over the past decade.

For the year 2023, EastGroup reported a 17% increase in full-year sales to $571 million, with FFO reported at $349 million, equal to $7.70 per share, with roughly two-thirds of this FFO metric paid out largely in the form of a dividend. Shares traded around 20 times FFO, while a dividend yield just in excess of 3% was paid out to investors.

With 47 million shares trading at $160 per share, the market value of the firm stood at $7.5 billion, far exceeding the book value of $2.6 billion at the time as the premium was a bit too high, and cap rates were a bit too tight, in order to see appeal.

Expectations Come In

Over the past year, shares of the company have traded in a $150-$190 trading range, now trading in the middle of the range.

In February of this year, EastGroup reported a 7% increase in FFO to $8.35 per share for the year 2024, guiding for further advancement to $8.80-$9.00 per share in 2025. This came after full-year revenues were up 12% to $640 million, although this came amidst dilution, with the share count up 8% on the year before, thereby reducing growth on a per-share basis.

This came after substantial growth of the portfolio during the year, including $390 million of net purchase during the year and about a quarter of a billion from own development activity.

The balance sheet totaled $5.1 billion in assets, with the book value of equity reported at $3.3 billion. With 50 million units trading at $166, the market value came in at $8.3 billion, a $5 billion premium over book value. This tells us that the market values these assets closer to $10 billion (with almost all assets relating to property) which implies a roughly 6.4% cap rate based on rental revenues of $640 million.

In April, the company reported a solid start to the year with revenues up 13% to $174 million, although growth was in part continued financed with dilution, as the unit count rose to 52 million units. Adjusted FFO rose by fourteen cents to $2.12 per share, as much of the headline growth has been offset by dilution of the shareholder base.

Similar trends continue with regard to the premium, as was the case based on the 2024 results. With revenues now trending near $700 million per annum, the implied cap rates come in a lot closer to 7% here, which is starting to look a bit more compelling, while the full year FFO is seen at around $8.94 per share.

Concluding Thoughts

With the dividend now payable at $1.40 per share, each quarter, these payouts have been paid out uninterruptedly for 32 years, having been hiked in 29 of those years, with the yield now standing at a decent 3.3%.

The company continues to benefit from the fact that cap rates come in at near 7% based on the market valuation, but the re-leasing spreads are huge due to market rental growth. This means that expiry of old leases drive growth, as evident by over 5% cash same-store NOI in the first quarter, even amidst slightly falling occupancy levels.

This driver continues and is nice given the uncertainty around tariffs and alike, although low usage of leverage is very comfortable, and this also includes reliance on outstanding forward equity sales. All this makes me quite upbeat here, in the sense that the company continues to post FFO growth from here onwards and likely higher dividends as well.

Given this, I think that EastGroup is likely a recent store of value in the long run, yet as is the case with so many REITs is that conviction is often hard to find, as the company too has to compete with yields offered on risk-free, or perceived risk-free securities.

This makes shares likely a fair play, but with growth still pursued at slightly to tight cap rates in my view, I am erring cautious here.

data.title: From Mining To Megawatts -- Core Scientific's High-Density Strategy Unfolds
data.description:
In our previous report we argued the investment case of Core Scientific. We highlighted how CORZ transformed itself from a struggling bitcoin miner to a key AI -Infrastructure with the help of the contracts and capex commitments that they secured form CoreWeave. We also highlighted why this investment opportunity existed, as two major events disrupted the stock price with one disrupting the whole AI sector and the other one impacting only Core Scientific. First news was the DeepSeek announcement - a new AI framework claiming significantly lower development costs and computation needs. This was refuted by the industry experts. The demand for AI computational power was further reaffirmed when CoreWeave announced a new contract with OpenAI. The second news of Microsoft pulling back on some commitments with CoreWeave also turned out to be false. After a stellar CoreWeave debut on the markets and no news of Microsoft contract cancellations it is clear that the contracts of CORZ with CoreWeave are secure. This was further reaffirmed in this earnings when CORZ confirmed that its contracts with CoreWeave are “Take-or-pay” and there is no ability to unilaterally terminate the contracts. This will be discussed in more detail as we go through the earnings.

Core Scientific’s Q1 2025 earnings reflected a strategic pivot from digital asset mining to becoming a dominant player in AI driven, high density colocation infrastructure. The quarter underscored rapid operational progress, strong strategic poisoning, and expanding enterprise demand amid a foundational partnership with CoreWeave.

CEO Adam Sullivan emphasized that Corz is at the forefront of one of the most significant shifts in digital infrastructure specifically designed for high performance computing, particularly GPU based workloads essential for AI and machine learnings. Corz is not merely participating in but leading the transformation. The company is currently the only pure play public infrastructure provider solely focused on high density colocation which gives it a unique competitive advantage.

CEO Sullivan also note that AI infrastructure demand remains robust, countering media narratives suggesting a softening. Traditional datacentres are struggling to keep up with the density requirements that AI workload demands. Core Scientific is capitalizing on that supply demand mismatch which gives them a real opportunity to lead and to carve out a meaningful share in the market that’s just getting started.

CoreWeave Partnership as a strategic growth engine

At the centre of core scientific growth strategy is its contracts with CoreWeave. Described as one of the largest GPU infrastructure agreements in the market, After a financial times article which claimed strained relationship between Microsoft and CoreWeave there was a huge debate about the nature of the contracts that Core Scientific has with CoreWeave. This quarter it has been clarified that these are take or pay contracts. This take or pay fixed price deal secures long term recurring revenue while keeping Core Scientific’s capital outlay minimal. CoreWeave is funding nearly all CapEx allowing Core Scientific to scale without overleveraging their balance sheet. The clarification around the CoreWeave contracts has eliminated the risk of contract cancellations if the computational demand is low.

The Denton, Texas facility emerged as the crown jewel of this partnership. Once completed, Denton will potentially house one of the largest GPU clusters in North America. The facility is already operational with 8MW delivered in MAY and additional 40MW expected in by Quarter end. CEO Adam Sullivan framed the speed of execution-achieving this within roughly four months of breaking ground-as a key differentiator.

Notably, OpenAI has been reported to be working with CoreWeave at Denton, underscoring the strategic importance and credibility of the site.

Strategic Goals

The company has announced three strategic priorities that are going to act as very positive catalysts as these are achieved.

Diversification of Customer Base

A core pillar of Core Scientific’s forward-looking strategy has been defined as reducing dependency on CoreWeave. Currently, CoreWeave represents a substantial portion of billable capacity, but the company aims to bring this below 50% by 2028. To that end, Core Scientific is actively expanding its customer pipeline.

While no new customer has been signed yet, the pipeline includes hyperscalers and significantly, a new front: large enterprises exploring AI infrastructure for the first time. Sullivan noted that enterprises clients are advancing more quickly through the decision-making cycle then hyper scalers and could become anchor tenants for future site developments. These customers are considering deployments or 50-100 MW, with a potential to grow beyond that, rivalling CoreWeave in scale over time. CoreWeave’s presence is a big credibility booster for these deals. Successful execution in Denton and other sites is expected to build trust and drive confidence across the market.

Executing on the CoreWeave contracts

Matt Brown, COO provided detailed updates on the construction progress. The company is simultaneously developing 570 MW across dour sties: Denton, Dalton, Marble, and Muskogee. These projects represent a combined infrastructure investment exceeding %5 billion.

At Denton, just to get an idea of the construction scale, over 600 contractors have logged 187,000-man hours poured 2800+ cubic yards of concrete, and installed over 30 miles of conduit. The company has already secured all equipment necessary to meet its 2025 delivery goals and is insulated from global tariff changes due to early procurement.

Looking ahead, Core scientific expects deliver 250 MW to CoreWeave by the end of 2025 and 590 MW by early 2027.

Expanding Data Centre Capacity

The company is targeting 300 MW of organic expansion across existing sites by 2027, supported by strong relationships with local utilities. An additional 400 is expected from new sites under development over the next three years.

Core Scientific is also exploring strategic M&A opportunities to extend its leadership in high density colocation. While most targets are expected to align with the company’s GPY focused assets o diversify workloads. The aim is to create a balanced portfolio while preserving efficiency and long-term value creation.

Notably, the Grand Forks site in North Dakota is being prepared for high density use, The company recently acquired 70 adjacent acres, with interest already emerging from hyperscalers, content providers, and enterprise clients.

The New CFO Jim Nygaard has deep experience in M&A, and capital markets. His addition is a very positive step in achieving the strategic expansion of data centre capacities through M&A’s.

Financial Strategy, Performance, & Tariffs

New CFO Jim Nygaard, replacing Denise Sterling, used the call to share his perspective on capital discipline. CoreWeave’s capex contributions have allowed core scientific to maintain a light balance sheet. As of Q1 the company held 780 million in liquidity including bitcoin. The long-term target for net debt to adjusted EBITDA is 4x consistent with peers.

Capital Deployment will be focused on:

  • Expanding capacity at existing sties,
  • Developing new high-density campuses, and
  • Targeted M&A aligned with strategic goals

Revenue for Q1 2025 was $79.5 million, a 16% sequential decline due to site conversions from bitcoin mining to colocation. Adjusted EBITDA came in at -$6.1m. The company mined 719 bitcoins in Q1 versus 974 in Q4 2024. The company also confirmed it ill stop reporting monthly bitcoin production, reflecting its shift away from mining. The focus now is on reporting construction progress tied to colocation projects.

The company also addressed the potential impacts of tariffs on data centre equipment. Management expects a 5% to 10% increase in CapEx for new builds, but believes the lease rates will rise proportionally, preserving margins. Core Scientifics early procurement strategy has largely insulated its 2025 plans from these impacts.

Interesting the company sees tariffs as a competitive advantage, likely to raise barriers for late entrant and further solidify its leadership in the high-density segment.

Conclusion

Core Scientific’s Q1 2025 results demonstrated a company in the midst of a bold and effective transformation. With the decline of its legacy Bitcoin mining business, CORZ is successfully repositioning itself as a high-impact infrastructure provider for the AI era. The company has not only identified a massive market opportunity but has taken decisive steps to lead it secured through strategic partnerships, accelerated project execution, and disciplined capital management.

The CoreWeave relationship remains the cornerstone of this transformation, and this quarter has put to rest the key investor concerns regarding contract durability and financial exposure. The take-or-pay structure, coupled with minimal capital outlay by Core Scientific, means the company can scale rapidly with limited balance sheet risk. Execution at the Denton facility further supports CORZ’s claim of being a first mover with operational capability in deploying large-scale, high-density GPU cluster.

Equally important is the company’s forward-looking strategy to diversify its customer base. With large enterprises now emerging as serious buyers of AI infrastructure, Core Scientific is building a healthy pipeline that could rival the scale of its CoreWeave engagement. Success here would not only reduce revenue concentration risk but also further elevate CORZ’s position as a mission critical infrastructure provider.

Financially, while Q1 shows a revenue dip and negative EBITDA due to the mining to colocation transition, the company maintains strong liquidity and is deploying capital with precision. The proactive approach to mitigate tariffs impacts, paired with a clear M&A roadmap under new CFO Jim Nygaard, gives confidence in the company’s ability to sustain long term capital efficient growth.

In short, Core Scientific is no longer a turnaround story. It is now a growth platform with deep exposure to one of the most transformative trends in technology. For investors seeking scalable AI infrastructure exposure with asymmetric upside potential, CORZ remains a compelling and timely opportunity. The clarity provided this quarter on the durability of the CoreWeave contracts, coupled with company’s execution strength and accelerating enterprise demand, has only reinforced our investment thesis. Our valuation framework and long position in Core Scientific remain firmly intact.
